patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH v3] btrfs: btrfs_log_dev_io_error() on all bio errors

version_history.subject: [PATCH v2] btrfs: btrfs_log_dev_io_error() on all bio errors
[PATCH v3] btrfs: btrfs_log_dev_io_error() on all bio errors

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch modifies the btrfs_log_dev_io_error() function to log device I/O errors for all bio error status codes, not just BLK_STS_IOERR and BLK_STS_TARGET. The change is motivated by the observation that some block layer drivers return unexpected status values, leading to incorrect conclusions about system behavior.

review_comments.author: Christoph Hellwig
review_comments.summary: Approved the patch with minor suggestions for improvement, specifically recommending the use of a switch statement to handle different error status codes more clearly.
review_comments.raw_body:
On Mon, Apr 06, 2026 at 09:15:15AM -0700, Boris Burkov wrote:
> As far as I can tell, we never intentionally constrained ourselves to
> these status codes, and it is misleading and surprising to lack the
> bdev error logging when we get a different error code from the block
> layer. This can lead to jumping to a wrong conclusion like "this
> system didn't see any bio failures but aborted with EIO".
> 
> For example on nvme devices, I observe many failures coming back as
> BLK_STS_MEDIUM. It is apparent that the nvme driver returns a variety of
> BLK_STS_* status values in nvme_error_status().
> 
> So handle the known expected errors and make some noise on the rest
> which we expect won't really happen.

Looks good:

Reviewed-by: Christoph Hellwig <hch@lst.de>

although now that you have an unlikely annotation for the non-ok
slow patch I'd use a switch statement for clarity int the second
pass.

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH v2 5/5] btrfs: cap shrink_delalloc iterations to 128M

version_history.subject: [PATCH v2 5/5] btrfs: cap shrink_delalloc iterations to 128M

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch limits the iterations of the `shrink_delalloc` function in Btrfs to prevent it from blocking indefinitely, especially when dealing with large amounts of writeback data. The function now iterates over delalloc work in units of 128MiB, rather than trying to reclaim all available space at once. This change aims to strike a balance between making progress on delalloc and not starving other types of reservations, which can lead to system stalls and long commit times.

review_comments.author: Filipe Manana
review_comments.summary: Reviewer noted that the patch is generally good, but suggested moving a variable declaration inside the loop instead of keeping it at the top level.
review_comments.raw_body:
We can also move the 'items' declaration inside this loop instead of top level.

Otherwise:

Reviewed-by: Filipe Manana <fdmanana@suse.com>

Thanks.

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH v2 4/5] btrfs: make inode->outstanding_extents a u64

version_history.subject: [PATCH v2 4/5] btrfs: make inode->outstanding_extents a u64

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch updates the data type of the 'outstanding_extents' field in the Btrfs inode structure from an unsigned integer (u32) to a 64-bit unsigned integer (u64). This change is motivated by the fact that the maximum file size is limited by LLONG_MAX, which is approximately 2^63. As a result, the maximum extent size computation in Btrfs has always been bounded above by this value, making it necessary to use a larger data type to accurately represent this value. The patch also updates several functions and variables to work with the new u64 data type.

review_comments.author: Filipe Manana
review_comments.summary: Reviewer requested the addition of extended assertions in the code to provide more informative error messages when certain conditions are not met.
review_comments.raw_body:
Please use extended assertions that print the checked values, so that
we can have useful output to debug if they fail.

Thanks.

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH v2 3/5] btrfs: account for compression in delalloc extent reservation

version_history.subject: [PATCH 3/5] btrfs: account for compression in delalloc extent reservation
[PATCH v2 3/5] btrfs: account for compression in delalloc extent reservation

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch addresses an issue where the btrfs kernel module under-reserved space for delayed reference reservations during delalloc writes when compression is enabled. The problem arises from an incorrect estimate of outstanding extents due to a mismatch between maximum uncompressed and compressed extent sizes. To fix this, the patch introduces new functions in btrfs_inode.h to calculate the maximum extent size based on inode properties and uses these values to accurately reserve space for delayed references. As a result, the patch improves the accuracy of delalloc reservation calculations and prevents under-reservation issues even when compression is forced or always enabled for an inode.

review_comments.author: Filipe Manana
review_comments.summary:
Reviewer Filipe Manana requested that the patch adhere to the kernel's coding style, specifically capitalizing the first word of each sentence and ensuring sentences are punctuated.

The reviewer suggested making a function u64 btrfs_inode_max_extent_size() static because it is only used within inode.c and does not need to be exported.
review_comments.raw_body:
Please follow our coding style: the first word is capitalized and
sentence should finish with punctuation.

---

There's no need to export this, make it static since it's not used
outside inode.c.

Thanks.

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH v2 2/5] btrfs: account for csum delayed_refs in delalloc

version_history.subject: [PATCH 2/5] btrfs: account for csum delayed_refs in delalloc
[PATCH v2 2/5] btrfs: account for csum delayed_refs in delalloc

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch series addresses issues in the Btrfs delalloc space management, specifically accounting for checksum delayed references during delalloc operations. The first patch in the series introduces a new function to calculate the reserved space for csum tree updates, while subsequent patches address additional concerns and edge cases.

review_comments.author: Filipe Manana
review_comments.summary: Identified two missing cases for csum tree updates during delalloc operations: COWing blocks from subvolume trees and raid stripe trees. Suggested adding reservations for these cases, as well as skipping the reservation if writing into a nocow/nosum inode.
review_comments.raw_body:
On Fri, Apr 3, 2026 at 11:29 PM Boris Burkov <boris@bur.io> wrote:
>
> As ordered_extents complete, they not only produce direct delayed refs,
> they also add csums via add_pending_csums. This produces some number of
> metadata delayed_refs as the csum tree is cow-ed. These refs are counted
> against the trans_handle delayed_rsv, thanks to trans->adding_csums.
>
> As a result, just like we account for the extent tree and free space
> tree when reserving delayed_refs for delalloc, we must also reserve for
> the csum tree. This is mirrored by the non-delayed-ref metadata
> reservation already accounting for csums.
>
> This serves to ensure we have a proper worst case estimate for
> delayed_rsv from delalloc.
>
> Signed-off-by: Boris Burkov <boris@bur.io>
> ---
>  fs/btrfs/delalloc-space.c | 13 ++++++++++---
>  1 file changed, 10 insertions(+), 3 deletions(-)
>
> diff --git a/fs/btrfs/delalloc-space.c b/fs/btrfs/delalloc-space.c
> index a087be55409f..c2e5c7758ed5 100644
> --- a/fs/btrfs/delalloc-space.c
> +++ b/fs/btrfs/delalloc-space.c
> @@ -252,12 +252,19 @@ static void btrfs_inode_rsv_release(struct btrfs_inode *inode, bool qgroup_free)
>
>  /*
>   * Each delalloc extent could become an ordered_extent and end up inserting a
> - * new extent into the extent and free space trees. So we must reserve
> - * delayed ref space up front for that.
> + * new extent into the extent and free space trees. It also may end up writing
> + * csums, so we must reserve delayed ref space up front for all of that.

Ok, this patch addresses my comment in the previous patch regarding
the missing accounting for csum tree updates.

But it lacks accounting for new delayed refs when COWing blocks from:

1) A subvolume tree (insert a new file extent item, or modify an
existing one in case of prealloc writes).

2) Raid stripe tree.


>   */
>  static u64 delalloc_calc_delayed_refs_rsv(const struct btrfs_fs_info *fs_info, u64 nr_extents)
>  {
> -       return btrfs_calc_delayed_ref_bytes(fs_info, nr_extents);
> +       /*
> +        * btrfs_calc_delayed_ref_bytes() does not assume csums as it is
> +        * also called from metadata writing contexts. So the extra
> +        * metadata insertion reservation represents the possibility of
> +        * delayed refs during csum tree writes.
> +        */
> +       return btrfs_calc_delayed_ref_bytes(fs_info, nr_extents) +
> +               btrfs_calc_insert_metadata_size(fs_info, nr_extents);

We can also skip the reservation for the csum tree if we are writing
into a nocow/nosum inode.
Check the rest of delalloc-space.c: there's an optimization I did
years ago to reduce space reservations in case the inode has the
BTRFS_INODE_NODATASUM flag set.

Thanks.

>  }
>
>  static void btrfs_calculate_inode_block_rsv_size(struct btrfs_fs_info *fs_info,
> --
> 2.53.0
>
>

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH v2 1/5] btrfs: reserve space for delayed_refs in delalloc

version_history.subject: [PATCH 1/5] btrfs: reserve space for delayed_refs in delalloc
[PATCH v2 1/5] btrfs: reserve space for delayed_refs in delalloc

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch addresses a problem where the Btrfs file system does not reserve enough space for delayed references (delayed_refs) during delalloc operations. Delalloc uses a per-inode block reservation to anticipate metadata needs, but it does not account for delayed refs. As a result, when writeback occurs and delayed refs are generated, they draw from an under-reserved trans_handle's delayed_refs_rsv, leading to incorrect assessments of reserved space in the metadata space reclaim logic. The patch fixes this by reserving space for delayed refs during delalloc operations.

review_comments.author: Filipe Manana
review_comments.summary:
Reviewer noted that delayed refs are created for multiple trees (extent, free space, checksum, and raid stripe) and requested accounting for nr_extents * 2 + additional trees

Reviewer Filipe Manana noted that the function's return type should be changed to void because none of its callers care about the return value.

Reviewer noted that writing into a prealloc extent can also generate delayed refs, which are not currently reserved in the delalloc logic.

Reviewer noted that the patch introduces a formatting inconsistency by using curly braces for single-statement ifs, which goes against the kernel's style preference.
review_comments.raw_body:
Ok, so that's 2 delayed refs, one to modify the extent tree and
another to modify the free space tree.
So below, techically we should account for nr_extents * 2.

And we actually need to modify more trees: the csum tree if we have
csums to insert and the new raid stripe tree.

---

Why return the number of bytes?
None of the callers cares about the return value, it can be just void.

---

So for the other case, writing into a prealloc extent, we should also
migrate space space because we will update the fs tree to modify a
file extent item (the call to btrfs_mark_extent_written()) and that
can generate delayed refs if we need to COW blocks from the fs tree
for that.

---

Another style change and this one goes against our style preference -
no { } for single statement if.

This is just a quick pass over the change, I'll look into the
following patches soon.

review_comments.author: Boris Burkov (author)
review_comments.summary: Author acknowledged a minor issue with style changes in the original patch, promising to double-check the full patch output and undo any unnecessary modifications.
review_comments.raw_body:
Sorry for the leaked style changes. Those were places I modified in the
first try using a new block_rsv and I didn't properly undo them while
changing it to the new migration design. I should have double checked
the full patch output for silly stuff like that.

Thanks for the review,
Boris

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH v2 0/5] btrfs: improve stalls under sudden writeback

version_history.subject: [PATCH 0/5] btrfs: improve stalls under sudden writeback
[PATCH v2 0/5] btrfs: improve stalls under sudden writeback

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch series addresses stalls caused by sudden writeback in Btrfs file systems on large memory systems. The patches improve reservations for delayed refs and smooth out the work in flush_space() to prevent system stalling due to excessive ordered extents and delayed refs blocking metadata space system operations. The changes include updating rsv accounting, cleaning up types counting max extents, reducing the unit of work in shrink_delalloc(), and capping iterations to 128M. These modifications aim to reduce stalls on large systems with high memory and disk capacity.

review_comments.author: Filipe Manana
review_comments.summary:
The reviewer pointed out that the patch should account for two delayed refs per extent, one for modifying the extent tree and another for the free space tree, as well as additional trees such as the csum tree and raid stripe tree.

Reviewer Filipe Manana pointed out that the function should return void instead of an integer count because none of its callers rely on the return value.

Filipe Manana noted that writing into a prealloc extent can also generate delayed refs due to COW operations, and requested migration of space space in this case.

Reviewer Filipe Manana pointed out that the patch introduces a style inconsistency by using curly braces for single-statement if blocks, which goes against the kernel's coding style preference.
review_comments.raw_body:
Ok, so that's 2 delayed refs, one to modify the extent tree and
another to modify the free space tree.
So below, techically we should account for nr_extents * 2.

And we actually need to modify more trees: the csum tree if we have
csums to insert and the new raid stripe tree.

---

Why return the number of bytes?
None of the callers cares about the return value, it can be just void.

---

So for the other case, writing into a prealloc extent, we should also
migrate space space because we will update the fs tree to modify a
file extent item (the call to btrfs_mark_extent_written()) and that
can generate delayed refs if we need to COW blocks from the fs tree
for that.

---

Another style change and this one goes against our style preference -
no { } for single statement if.

This is just a quick pass over the change, I'll look into the
following patches soon.

review_comments.author: Filipe Manana
review_comments.summary:
The reviewer noted that the patch lacks accounting for delayed refs created when COWing blocks from subvolume and raid stripe trees, which was previously identified as an issue in another patch.

Reviewer Filipe Manana suggested that the patch could be improved by skipping the reservation for the csum tree when writing into a nocow/nosum inode, and also mentioned an existing optimization in delalloc-space.c that reduces space reservations for such inodes.
review_comments.raw_body:
Ok, this patch addresses my comment in the previous patch regarding
the missing accounting for csum tree updates.

But it lacks accounting for new delayed refs when COWing blocks from:

1) A subvolume tree (insert a new file extent item, or modify an
existing one in case of prealloc writes).

2) Raid stripe tree.

---

We can also skip the reservation for the csum tree if we are writing
into a nocow/nosum inode.
Check the rest of delalloc-space.c: there's an optimization I did
years ago to reduce space reservations in case the inode has the
BTRFS_INODE_NODATASUM flag set.

Thanks.

review_comments.author: Boris Burkov (author)
review_comments.summary: Author acknowledged a minor issue with style changes in the patch, promising to be more careful in the future.
review_comments.raw_body:
Sorry for the leaked style changes. Those were places I modified in the
first try using a new block_rsv and I didn't properly undo them while
changing it to the new migration design. I should have double checked
the full patch output for silly stuff like that.

Thanks for the review,
Boris

review_comments.author: Filipe Manana
review_comments.summary:
Reviewer Filipe Manana pointed out that the patch description does not conform to the kernel's coding style, specifically stating that the first word of each sentence should be capitalized and sentences should end with punctuation.

Reviewer Filipe Manana noted that the function in question is only used within inode.c and suggested making its declaration static to prevent unnecessary exports.
review_comments.raw_body:
Please follow our coding style: the first word is capitalized and
sentence should finish with punctuation.

---

There's no need to export this, make it static since it's not used
outside inode.c.

Thanks.

review_comments.author: Filipe Manana
review_comments.summary: Reviewer Filipe Manana requested the patch author to add extended assertions that print the checked values for easier debugging when they fail.
review_comments.raw_body:
Please use extended assertions that print the checked values, so that
we can have useful output to debug if they fail.

Thanks.

review_comments.author: Filipe Manana
review_comments.summary: Reviewer suggested a minor improvement to code organization by moving the 'items' declaration inside a loop, rather than having it at the top level.
review_comments.raw_body:
We can also move the 'items' declaration inside this loop instead of top level.

Otherwise:

Reviewed-by: Filipe Manana <fdmanana@suse.com>

Thanks.
